I’ll pull the Linear project context first, then inspect the repo around the staged push/domain boundary so the comparison is grounded in both sources. After that I’ll give you a code-review-style read: what matches, what’s still off, and where I’d be careful.

Linear has no issues or milestones under that project; the useful context is the project description plus one audit comment. I’m now checking whether the code still has the dependency inversion described there, and whether the suggested boundary matches the actual module shape.

content.text: The project concern is still present: `domain/repo_actions.rs` and `domain/projection_views.rs` both call `crate::git::import::*`. I also found that part of the proposed cleanup may already have happened: `StoredRepository` now owns `live_tree()`, so I’m checking whether `git/import/staging.rs` still reaches into HTTP/app state or whether that part is stale.

Current `staging.rs` no longer imports `http::responses::repo_owner_ids`, so that exact part of the Linear description is stale. It still imports `state::live_tree`, but `state::live_tree` is just a wrapper over `StoredRepository::live_tree()`, so the real remaining architectural issue is the domain-to-git dependency plus some staged-push domain behavior still physically living under Git import.

The tests are already exercising the behavior the project cares about: staging without changing live tree, apply/reject, visibility effects, projection behavior, and cleanup side effects. The refactor should mostly move where those rules live, not invent new coverage from scratch.

The dependency scan is narrow: only three domain lines import outward, all for staged update validation/application. That’s a good sign for this Linear item as a one-PR refactor, because the acceptance check is not masking a wider domain-layer cleanup.

content.text:
I think the Linear project is still valid and worth doing, but its description is slightly stale.

The real remaining problem is exactly the important one: domain code still calls into Git import code at [repo_actions.rs:226](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/api/src/domain/repo_actions.rs:226), [repo_actions.rs:245](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/api/src/domain/repo_actions.rs:245), and [projection_views.rs:86](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/api/src/domain/projection_views.rs:86). That means staged-update policy validation and application are owned by `api/src/git/import/staging.rs`, even though they mutate `StoredRepository`, create logical commits, apply visibility rules, emit visibility events, and bump change versions. That is domain behavior.

The stale bit: `StoredRepository::live_tree()` already exists at [store.rs:371](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/api/src/domain/store.rs:371). `git/import/staging.rs` still imports `state::live_tree`, but that wrapper only calls `repo.live_tree()`. I did not find the `http::responses::repo_owner_ids` dependency mentioned in Linear, so that part appears already cleaned up.

My recommended shape:

- Add `api/src/domain/staged_updates.rs`.
- Move `apply_receive_pack_update`, `validate_staged_update_policy`, `staged_visibility_rule`, and the apply-time helper logic out of [git/import/staging.rs:155](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/api/src/git/import/staging.rs:155).
- Rename away from `receive_pack` in domain, probably something like `apply_staged_update_to_repo`, because receive-pack is delivery mechanism language.
- Keep Git branch validation, Git repo parsing, artifact reading, object-store uploads, and line-diff calculation in `git/import`.
- Have `repo_actions.rs`, `projection_views.rs`, and `git/import/staging.rs` all call the domain module instead of Git import being the owner of those rules.
- Replace `state::live_tree(repo)` in staging with `repo.live_tree()` or let the moved domain code own that directly.

Risk is moderate, not because the change is large, but because the helper currently has subtle behavior: the low-level apply function does not clear `repo.staged_update`; `repo_actions::apply_staged_update` does. The refactor should preserve that distinction, plus deletion visibility behavior and cleanup effects.

Coverage looks pretty good already. The existing `git_receive` and `review_publish` tests cover staging, apply/reject, visibility, projection, and cleanup behavior. I’d mostly move/rename tests as needed and add an `rg`-style boundary check to the PR notes: no `api/src/domain/**` references to `crate::git`, `crate::http`, `crate::state`, `crate::db`, or persistence.

I did not run cargo tests since this was a comparison pass, not a code change. Static scan confirms the boundary violation is currently narrow and fixable in one focused PR.

content.text: I’m going a bit further than the minimal import swap: the staging decision and staged-update construction are also domain rules, while Git import can supply the parsed file changes and line diff callback. input.comment: Addressed in 6393497: `staged_updates.rs` no longer imports or returns `ApiError`. It now exposes a staged-update domain error, and the current Git/API callers translate that to `ApiError` at the adapter edge. Existing broader domain modules still use `ApiError`, but this PR no longer adds that coupling in the new staged update boundary.

---
name: control-in-app-browser
description: "Control the in-app Browser. Use to open, navigate, inspect, test, click, type, screenshot, or verify local targets such as localhost, 127.0.0.1, ::1, file://, the current in-app browser tab, and websites shown side by side inside Codex."
---

# Browser

Use this skill for browser automation tasks such as inspecting pages, navigating, testing local apps, clicking, typing, taking screenshots, and reading visible page state.

If this plugin is listed as available in the session, treat that as mandatory reading before browser work. Open and follow this skill before saying that Browser is unavailable and before falling back to standalone Playwright or Computer Use.

Do not skip this skill just because Computer Use MCP tool calls are directly visible or appear easier to invoke. The presence of Computer Use tools is not evidence that Computer Use is the preferred browser surface.

## Setup Documentation

Use `await agent.documentation.get("bootstrap-troubleshooting")` when browser setup succeeds but discovery or selection fails.

## Bootstrap

These setup details are internal. User-facing progress updates should be less technical in nature. Never mention `Node REPL`, `node_repl`, `REPL`, JavaScript sessions, module exports, reading documentation, or loading instructions unless a user is asking for that exact information. If setup or recovery is needed, describe it naturally as connecting to the browser or retrying the browser connection.

The `browser-client` module is the core entry point for browser use, and is available under `scripts/browser-client.mjs` in this plugin's root directory. ALWAYS import it using an absolute path. IMPORTANT: If this path cannot be found, stop and report that this plugin is missing `scripts/browser-client.mjs`. NEVER use the built in `browser-client` library.

Run browser setup code through the Node REPL `js` tool. In this environment the callable tool id typically appears as `mcp__node_repl__js`. If it is not already available, use tool discovery for `node_repl js` without setting a result limit. You need the `js` execution tool: `js_reset` only clears state, and `js_add_node_module_dir` only changes package resolution. Do not call either helper while trying to expose `js`. If `js` is still not available, search again for `node_repl js` with `limit: 10`.

Initialize the runtime once per fresh Node session, select the in-app browser, and immediately read its complete documentation:

```js
if (globalThis.agent?.browsers == null) {
  const { setupBrowserRuntime } = await import("<plugin root>/scripts/browser-client.mjs");
  await setupBrowserRuntime({ globals: globalThis });
}
globalThis.browser = await agent.browsers.get("iab");
nodeRepl.write(await browser.documentation());
```

If setup succeeds but browser discovery or selection fails, read `await agent.documentation.get("bootstrap-troubleshooting")` before resetting the JavaScript session or trying another browser-control mechanism.

Use the browser bound to `browser` for tasks in this skill. Do not switch to another browser-control mechanism to work around authentication. If sign-in blocks requested navigation and the browser documentation does not provide a supported authentication flow, ask the user to sign in in the in-app browser and tell you when it is ready.

The ability to interact directly with the browser is exposed through the `browser-client` runtime via the `agent.browsers.*` API. Before trying to interact with it, you MUST emit and read the complete documentation returned by `await browser.documentation()` in one go. For the initial documentation read, run the exact direct call `nodeRepl.write(await browser.documentation());` shown above. Do not assign the documentation to a variable, inspect its length, slice it, truncate it, summarize it, or emit only an excerpt. Do not proactively split the documentation into pages or chunks. Only if the tool output itself explicitly reports that it was truncated may you emit and read smaller chunks until you have read the documentation in its entirety.

Only the Node REPL `js` tool (`mcp__node_repl__js`) can be used to control the in-app browser. Do not use external MCP browser-control tools, separate browser automation servers, or other browser skills for this surface. References to Playwright mean the in-skill `tab.playwright` API after browser-client setup.
